Work Description: As an accountant, my primary responsibilities include managing and maintaining accurate financial records for businesses or individuals. I prepare financial statements, such as balance sheets, income statements, and cash flow statements, to provide a clear overview of the organization’s financial health. I ensure compliance with tax laws by preparing and filing tax returns and advising on strategies to minimize tax liabilities. Additionally, I perform budgeting and forecasting to help organizations plan their finances effectively. I also conduct audits, analyze financial data, and offer recommendations to improve financial performance, ensuring all accounting activities adhere to regulatory standards and company policies.
Work Tasks: As an accountant, the tasks I typically spend the most time on include managing financial records and ensuring their accuracy. This involves recording transactions, reconciling accounts, and maintaining ledgers. A significant portion of my time goes toward preparing financial reports, such as income statements and balance sheets, which help stakeholders understand the financial position of the organization. I also dedicate time to tax preparation and compliance, including calculating liabilities, filing returns, and staying updated on changes in tax laws. Additionally, I work on budgeting and forecasting, conducting audits, and analyzing financial data to identify trends and opportunities for cost savings or efficiency improvements.
Work Tools:
The tools and software I use as an accountant are very important in managing financial tasks effectively. First on the list is accounting software, such as QuickBooks, Xero, or Sage, which I use daily. These tools handle essential tasks such as recording transactions, reconciling accounts, generating invoices, and managing payroll. They are the backbone of my work, ensuring financial records are accurate and up-to-date.

I also strongly rely on spreadsheet tools such as Microsoft Excel and Google Sheets. These are simply indispensable in composing financial models, doing detailed analysis of data, and arranging substantial volumes of data. Their flexibility makes them very extendable for tailored reporting and calculating to suit my needs, therefore a core tool within my workflow.

During tax season, tax software such as TurboTax, ProSeries, or Drake Tax becomes critical. These tools streamline the preparation and filing of tax returns, ensuring compliance with the latest tax laws and minimizing errors. In larger organizations, I frequently use enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems like SAP or Oracle NetSuite to integrate accounting with other business processes such as inventory and procurement.

For audit-related tasks, tools like CaseWare, IDEA, or ACL help me test internal controls, analyze financial risks, and conduct audits efficiently. Project management tools such as Trello, Asana, or Monday.com assist in tracking progress on accounting projects, ensuring deadlines are met, and enabling collaboration with team members. Lastly, document management systems like DocuSign or Adobe Acrobat are essential for handling digital signatures, organizing financial documents, and maintaining secure records.

Together, these tools streamline my workflow, enhance accuracy, and improve collaboration, enabling me to perform my tasks efficiently and effectively.
Work Processes:
Managing Financial Records
Managing financial records is a daily responsibility that ensures all transactions are accurately tracked and organized. My process begins with recording transactions, such as sales, purchases, and payments, into accounting software like QuickBooks or Xero. I categorize these transactions into the correct accounts to maintain a clear and consistent ledger. At the end of each day or week, I reconcile these records by comparing them with bank statements to verify their accuracy. Any discrepancies are investigated by reviewing supporting documents such as invoices or receipts. This process culminates in preparing for monthly or quarterly closings, ensuring the financial data is ready for reporting or audits.

Preparing Financial Reports
Preparing financial reports, such as income statements and balance sheets, is another key task. I begin by gathering and reviewing data from the accounting system to ensure that all transactions are recorded and categorized correctly. Using accounting software, I generate preliminary reports and customize them to emphasize specific metrics or performance indicators. Once the reports are generated, I carefully analyze the data, identify trends, and compare actual results to budgets or forecasts. This analysis helps me spot inconsistencies or areas needing improvement. Finally, I present the completed reports to stakeholders, providing clear explanations of key insights and answering any questions they may have.

Tax Preparation and Compliance
Tax preparation is a crucial part of my work, especially during tax season. My process starts with gathering all necessary documentation, including invoices, receipts, payroll records, and prior tax returns. I enter this data into tax software like ProSeries or Drake Tax, ensuring every entry aligns with current tax regulations. I review deductions, credits, and other tax-related items to optimize the tax position while maintaining compliance. Once the return is prepared, I thoroughly review it for accuracy before filing it electronically or preparing it for submission by mail. Throughout this process, I stay updated on changes in tax laws to ensure accurate and compliant filings.

By following these structured processes, I ensure that my work is accurate, efficient, and compliant with regulatory requirements, helping my organization maintain financial integrity.

I check in with medical histories, prescribe medication, write the orders, and dictate tests. Also, I have to keep learning as various advancements concerning medicine come into the market; thus, trainings or researches may be gated for such advancements. All in all, it can be frenetic, but rather, it can be an enriching experience whereby you are constantly helping other human beings carry on with their lives healthily.
Work Tasks:
As a doctor, my everyday responsibilities may include:

Patient consultations: I spend hours treating, diagnosing, discussing symptoms, and suggesting treatment plans for patients.
Medical procedures: These depend on my specialty, so I may also be called upon to perform surgeries or procedures, all of which can be invasive, like suturing, obtaining samples for tests, or some other medical intervention.
Generating patient charts: After consultations and procedures, I take time to document an accurate diagnosis, treatment plan, and subsequent changes in care on patient records.
Collaboration with fellow professionals: I frequently collaborate with other healthcare staff-such as nurses, specialists, and other administrative personnel-to provide all-encompassing care to the patient.
Research and continuing education: I spend time keeping abreast of developments in the medical field through attendance at conferences, readership of medical journals, and the acquisition of new techniques or treatments.

Communication Tools (Teams, TigerText): Medium use for team communication.

Based on these designated instructions, I will then feed natural language prompts into one of our proprietary software programs, which uses the Python programming language to simulate the experience that a user of a tool like ChatGPT. Work Description: So, for my work and the way that I operate is that local attorneys will get requests for title searches from banks. Then the attorneys that I work for will then call me and tell me in the morning, I need a title search for a property at this address. And assuming that I have time, I'll say, great, and I'll take it. I typically offer a two week window from when I get the request to when it's finished, but there are a lot of days where I finish it the day that I get the request, but I like to give myself a buffer. Then once I'm working on a title search, I use the online database to pull up the current deed and then I start back tracing the deeds and I go back 25 years. I make a note of anything that's changed or different through the deeds, then I write up a summary and type a report and print off the most recent deed and then deliver a copy to the attorneys.
Work Tasks: So, once I've accepted a research request, I'll go into the online database and pull up the current deed to the property, and then I'll look back and find the last or previous conveyance and pull that up. Then I'll go through and compare any restrictions, signatures, date, discrepancies, and if that's all clear, then I'll move on and find the previous conveyance from that until I've gone back 25 years for that property. Once I get that done, I will type up a summary with my findings and print off the most recent deed and deliver it to the attorneys who will then review it, give their sign-off for it, and then take it and deliver it to the bank.
Work Tools: The only real software I use is the Tennessee Register of Deeds page because they have all of the deed books scanned in. So I can just look them all up online. And then the other software I use is going to be Microsoft Word which is where I'll type up my findings.
Work Processes: The task I spend the most time on is looking at previous conveyance. So that is pulling up the current deed, finding the previous conveyance, record book date and page. Then pulling up the record books, the warranty deed books. Going to the page and finding the previous one. That's probably the task I do the most. The second task is going to be to type up a summary with my findings of any discrepancies or red flags between those two. And that is just listing out what I've looked at and then making a notation in the margin of what looks off and why I think it's off. And the third task that I do is just going to be assembling my packets and delivering them to my clients.
AI Envision: I could see myself using AI to write up my summaries of my findings but as far as the other parts of my job I don't think they are replaceable by any AI function because I'm pulling deeds that are handwritten things are not scanned by text they're just scanned by photo so there's no way for AI to search through that and also there's a certain element of human error that AI just simply can't catch as well as a human

- Microsoft Excel: My primary tool for technical and economic analysis, including production trend evaluations, variance analyses, cost tracking, and financial modeling (e.g., DCF, NPV, IRR). Also used for generating graphs and data visualizations.

- Microsoft Word: Used extensively for documenting procedures, technical reports, feasibility studies, and project proposals. I created and standardized operating procedures for well operations and artificial lift systems.

Work Processes:
Example for production optimization procedure.
1. Identifying the Problematic Well
The process often begins by reviewing automated production data or receiving reports from offshore field personnel. I look for wells showing abnormal declines, erratic flow, or complete shutdowns.

2. Analyzing the Problem & Formulating Solutions
Using PipeSim, I simulate well performance under different artificial lift and flow scenarios to diagnose the issue, whether it’s equipment failure, reservoir pressure drop, or flow assurance problems. I support this with Excel to visualize trends and estimate impact.

3. Designing the Procedure
Once the root cause is understood, I use Word to draft a clear technical procedure outlining both the primary solution and a backup plan, ensuring safety and feasibility in offshore execution.

4. Economic Evaluation
Using Excel, I estimate the potential economic benefit of applying the solution, whether through incremental production gains, cost savings, or avoiding prolonged downtime. I also assess the cost of inaction to justify urgency.

5. Proposing the Solution
Depending on the scale and budget:
Minor fixes are proposed via email with supporting attachments.
More complex or higher-budget procedures are submitted through a web-based internal system for formal review and approval.

6. Communication & Reporting
If needed, I prepare a PowerPoint presentation to brief senior engineers or stakeholders, summarizing the issue, analysis, proposed solutions, and expected outcomes.

7. Execution & Monitoring
Once approved, I send the finalized procedure to the offshore team via email and remain on-call via phone or email to provide real-time technical support during execution.

However, I believe experienced engineers should oversee, validate, and refine AI-generated insights, particularly for high-impact decisions involving significant cost or operational risk. In this hybrid model, AI boosts efficiency while engineers maintain oversight and accountability.

Work Description: As an Intelligence Analyst, my day-to-day work involves gathering, analyzing, and interpreting data from various sources to identify potential threats and provide actionable insights. I typically review reports, surveillance data, and digital communications to detect patterns or anomalies. I also prepare intelligence briefings and reports for decision-makers, ensuring they are well-informed about current and emerging risks. Collaboration with law enforcement, cybersecurity teams, and other analysts is a regular part of my job, as is staying up to date with geopolitical developments and emerging technologies that may impact security.
Work Tasks:
In my role as an Intelligence Analyst, I usually start the day by reviewing reports and updates from various sources. A lot of my time goes into collecting and monitoring data, trying to spot any unusual patterns or potential risks. I spend a good part of the day analyzing that information and putting the pieces together to form a bigger picture.

Once I have something meaningful, I work on writing reports or summaries to share with others—whether it's for internal teams or external partners. I also join meetings or discussions with colleagues to go over findings or collaborate on ongoing investigations. Sometimes I also use part of my time to read up on new developments in security, tech, or global affairs to stay sharp and informed.
Work Tools:
I use a mix of tools to get my work done, depending on the task. The ones I use most often are:

Excel / Google Sheets – I use these daily for organizing raw data, tracking patterns, and sometimes for basic analysis.

Open-source intelligence (OSINT) tools like Maltego, Shodan, and sometimes Google Dorks – I rely on these a lot for gathering information from public sources.

Threat intelligence platforms (such as Recorded Future or Anomali) – These help me stay updated on potential threats and ongoing incidents.

Report writing tools like Microsoft Word and PowerPoint – I use these regularly to put together findings and create presentations.

Communication tools – Email and secure messaging platforms like Signal or encrypted channels are essential for coordination.

Mapping or link analysis software like i2 Analyst’s Notebook – I use this occasionally when I need to visualize connections between people, events, or data points.

I also keep up with news and online sources to supplement what I get from the tools above.
Work Processes:
One of the tasks I spend the most time on is analyzing data to spot patterns or anything unusual. My process usually starts with gathering information from different sources—things like reports, databases, or public data online. I try to organize it all in a way that makes it easier to work with, usually using Excel or Google Sheets.

Once I have everything in place, I go through the data to clean it up—removing duplicates or anything that’s not useful. Then I start looking for connections or trends. If it’s a bit complex, I might use tools like Maltego to help visualize how things are linked.

After I’ve made sense of the information, I usually write a short report or summary, depending on who it’s for. I try to keep it clear and straight to the point, highlighting the most important things they need to know. If needed, I’ll include charts or visuals to make it easier to understand.
AI Envision:
I see AI becoming really useful in my daily work, especially for speeding up some of the more repetitive or time-consuming tasks. For example, AI could help with scanning large volumes of data quickly, flagging anything that stands out or looks suspicious. It could also assist with sorting and organizing information more efficiently so I can focus on the deeper analysis part.

I also think AI tools could be useful for summarizing reports, helping with quick drafts, or even generating visuals based on raw data. As long as the final judgment still comes from a human, I think AI could be a strong support tool to make the overall process faster and more accurate.

Work Description: As a Customs Broker, I help individuals and businesses ensure their imported and exported goods move smoothly across international borders. My main responsibilities include preparing and submitting documentation required by customs authorities, classifying goods according to tariff codes, and ensuring compliance with all import/export regulations. I also calculate duties, taxes, and fees, and work closely with clients, shipping companies, and government agencies to make sure everything is processed efficiently and legally. Ultimately, my job is to take the complexity out of international shipping and help clients avoid delays or penalties.
Work Tasks:
As a Customs Broker, my work involves a variety of tasks that ensure the smooth and legal movement of goods across international borders. Here's how I typically divide my time:

Documentation and Compliance (30-35%): A significant portion of my time is spent preparing and submitting customs documentation, including entry forms, invoices, and certificates of origin. I also ensure all shipments comply with relevant import/export regulations.

Classifying Goods and Calculating Duties (20-25%): I review product details to accurately classify items under the Harmonized Tariff Schedule and calculate duties, taxes, and fees based on country-specific rules.

Coordinating with Clients and Partners (20-25%): I regularly communicate with clients, freight forwarders, customs officers, and government agencies to ensure shipments are processed without delays.

Problem-Solving and Issue Resolution (10-15%): I handle customs holds, document discrepancies, and other issues that can delay clearance, working quickly to resolve problems and keep cargo moving.

Staying Current with Regulations (5-10%): I dedicate time to staying updated on changing trade laws, tariffs, and customs policies, which is crucial to maintaining compliance.

Every day brings new challenges, but these core tasks make up the bulk of my responsibilities.
Work Tools:
As a Customs Broker, I rely on several tools and software systems to carry out my daily tasks efficiently and stay compliant with regulations. Here’s a breakdown of the tools I use, sorted by how frequently I use them:

Customs Brokerage Software (Daily)

Examples: Descartes, WiseTech Global’s CargoWise, or Farrow
These platforms are essential for filing customs entries electronically, managing client data, generating shipping documents, and tracking declarations.

Government Portals (Daily)

Examples: ACE (Automated Commercial Environment) in the U.S., CBSA portal in Canada
I use these portals to submit filings, monitor shipment status, access import/export records, and ensure compliance with customs regulations.

HS Code Lookup Tools (Frequently)

Examples: WCO Harmonized System, CustomsInfo, or tariff lookup tools on government websites
These are vital for accurately classifying goods, which affects duties, taxes, and compliance.

Communication Tools (Frequently)

Examples: Email, Microsoft Teams, Slack, and phone systems
I use these to stay in close contact with clients, freight forwarders, customs officials, and logistics partners.

Accounting and Billing Software (Regularly)

Examples: QuickBooks, SAP, or in-system modules
These help track billing, invoices, and payments for customs services.

Document Management Systems (Occasionally)

Examples: SharePoint, Dropbox, or internal platforms
Used to store and organize shipping documents, licenses, permits, and compliance records.

These tools help me stay organized, efficient, and compliant while managing the complexities of international trade.
Work Processes:
One of the tasks I spend the most time on is preparing and submitting customs documentation for import shipments. Here’s a step-by-step breakdown of how I typically handle this process:

Receive Shipment Information
I start by collecting all necessary documents from the client or freight forwarder—this includes commercial invoices, packing lists, bills of lading, certificates of origin, and any special permits or licenses required for specific goods.

Review and Verify Documents
I thoroughly check all documents for accuracy and completeness. I confirm details like product descriptions, quantities, declared values, and country of origin to avoid customs delays or penalties.

Classify the Goods
I use HS code lookup tools or tariff databases to classify each product under the appropriate Harmonized Tariff Schedule code. This step is critical because it determines the duties and taxes payable.

Calculate Duties and Taxes
Based on the classification and declared value, I calculate the applicable import duties, VAT/GST, and any other government fees. If the client qualifies for trade agreements or exemptions, I apply them accordingly.

Data Entry into Customs System
Using customs brokerage software (like CargoWise or Descartes), I input all required data and prepare the electronic entry forms. The system generates the customs declaration, which is then submitted through the relevant government portal (like ACE in the U.S.).

Submit and Monitor the Entry
After submission, I monitor the status of the declaration. If customs flags the entry for review or inspection, I follow up promptly, provide additional documentation if needed, and work to resolve any issues.

Confirm Release and Notify Client
Once the shipment is cleared, I notify the client and provide them with the release documents. If payment of duties is required, I either arrange it directly or coordinate with the client to ensure it’s completed.

Recordkeeping and Compliance Check
I store all documents in our internal system to meet legal recordkeeping requirements and perform occasional compliance audits to make sure our filings are accurate and up to date.

This process is detail-heavy, but it’s essential for avoiding costly delays and keeping goods moving smoothly across borders.
AI Envision:
As a Customs Broker, I see AI playing a big role in streamlining and improving accuracy in my daily workflow. Here's how I envision using it:

Automated Document Review and Data Entry
AI could help extract and validate key information from invoices, packing lists, and other shipping documents, reducing the time spent on manual data entry and minimizing human error.

HS Code Classification Assistance
AI can analyze product descriptions and suggest the most accurate HS codes based on past entries, global trade databases, and compliance rules. This would save time and improve accuracy in tariff classification.

Real-Time Compliance Checks
AI tools could automatically flag potential compliance issues, such as missing permits, embargoed countries, or restricted goods, helping ensure shipments meet all regulations before submission.

Predictive Risk Management
By analyzing historical data and customs patterns, AI can help identify high-risk shipments that may be subject to inspection or delay, allowing us to proactively resolve issues before they arise.

Workflow Automation and Client Communication
AI-driven systems could automate routine follow-ups, status updates, and document requests with clients, improving service efficiency and keeping everyone informed in real time.

Overall, I see AI as a valuable assistant that enhances speed, accuracy, and compliance, while giving me more time to focus on problem-solving and client service.

Work Description: As molecular biologist, I  study the structure and function of molecules essential to life, such as DNA, RNA, and proteins, to understand how cells work and how genes are regulated.
Work Tasks: As a molecular biologist, I spend most of my time in the lab running experiments like extracting DNA, analyzing gene expression, or modifying genes to study their function. I also spend a good amount of time recording and analyzing data, reading research papers to stay updated, and writing reports or preparing for presentations. A smaller chunk of time goes into planning experiments and maintaining lab equipment. It’s a mix of hands-on work, critical thinking and constant learning.
Work Tools:
Sure! Here are the tools and software I use most frequently as a molecular biologist, sorted by how often I use them:

Pipettes and Centrifuges – These are daily essentials in the lab for preparing and processing samples.

PCR Machines (Thermal Cyclers) – Used frequently for amplifying DNA segments during experiments.

Gel Electrophoresis Equipment – Often used to analyze DNA, RNA, or protein samples after extraction or PCR.

Spectrophotometers/Nanodrop – Used regularly to measure the concentration and purity of nucleic acids and proteins.

Micropipette Calibration Software – Used for accuracy checks and maintenance.

Bioinformatics Software (e.g., BLAST, SnapGene, or Geneious) – I use these for designing primers, analyzing sequences, and comparing genetic data.

Excel/Google Sheets – For organizing and analyzing raw data.

GraphPad Prism/R or Python (pandas, matplotlib) – For statistical analysis and data visualization.

Reference Management Tools (Zotero, Mendeley) – Useful for organizing scientific literature during research.

Lab Notebook Software (like Benchling or LabArchives) – Used to digitally document experiments and track progress.

These tools help streamline everything from wet lab experiments to data analysis and research documentation.
Work Processes:
Absolutely! One of the tasks I spend the most time on is running molecular biology experiments, especially DNA extraction and PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction). Here’s a step-by-step walk-through of my typical process:

1. Planning the Experiment
I start by defining my objective: What gene or DNA segment am I targeting?

Then I select or design primers using SnapGene or BLAST to make sure they’ll bind specifically to my DNA of interest.

I list out reagents, prepare a protocol, and double-check concentrations and controls.

2. Sample Preparation
I collect and label samples.

Using micropipettes, I carefully extract DNA using a kit or manual protocol—this involves lysis, purification (usually with a spin column), and elution.

I use a Nanodrop or spectrophotometer to check the quality and quantity of the DNA.

3. Setting Up PCR
I prepare a PCR master mix, which includes Taq polymerase, dNTPs, primers, buffer, and template DNA.

I pipette everything into PCR tubes with precision to avoid contamination.

The tubes go into the thermal cycler, where the machine runs a programmed cycle of denaturation, annealing, and extension.

4. Analyzing PCR Results
After amplification, I load the samples into an agarose gel for gel electrophoresis.

I run the gel, stain it with ethidium bromide or SYBR Safe, and visualize bands under UV light.

The presence and size of bands tell me if the reaction worked and if my target DNA was amplified.

5. Data Recording & Analysis
I take images of the gel results and store them.

Then I document every step, condition, and outcome in my lab notebook or Benchling.

If needed, I quantify the bands using ImageJ and analyze the results further in Excel or GraphPad Prism.

6. Troubleshooting (if needed)
If something goes wrong (e.g., no bands or smears), I review reagent quality, primer design, or thermocycler settings, and repeat with adjustments.

This cycle can take a full day or more, depending on the complexity. It’s methodical, but super rewarding when it works.
AI Envision:
I see AI becoming a powerful assistant in my daily workflow as a molecular biologist in several ways:

Experimental Design & Primer Optimization
AI tools can suggest optimal primer sequences, predict secondary structures, and even flag potential off-targets faster and more accurately than manual searches.

Data Analysis & Interpretation
AI could automate image analysis from gel electrophoresis, quantify band intensity, or analyze gene expression data—saving hours of manual work.

Literature Review
With AI, I can quickly summarize the latest papers, extract key findings, and stay updated without combing through dozens of articles myself.

Lab Management
AI-powered lab management tools can track reagent levels, schedule equipment use, and even suggest reordering before I run out—keeping the workflow smooth.

Troubleshooting
AI can help diagnose failed experiments by analyzing patterns across multiple runs and suggesting the most likely causes and solutions.

Writing & Reporting
Drafting research reports, formatting references, or creating visuals for presentations could be sped up significantly with AI support.

In short, AI can help reduce the repetitive, time-consuming parts of molecular biology so I can focus more on discovery and innovation.

Typical Day-to-Day Work of a Library Assistant
Core Responsibilities

Work Tools: Microsoft Office Tools like  Excel, Word, PowerPoint, KOHA library Management Software, Librarika Software
Work Processes:
Shelving and Reshelving

Step-by-Step Guide: Shelving and Reshelving Books in the Library
1. Check-In and Sorting

When books are returned, they are checked in at the circulation or access services desk.

Staff sort returned books by collection and arrange them in call number order on a book cart or designated shelves behind the desk.

2. Preparing for Shelving

Place books on the top shelf of a book cart, spine up and in call number order.

Double-check that all items are properly checked in using the library’s management system.

3. Shelving Process

Take the cart to the correct section of the library.

Begin shelving from the top left of the first shelf in a bay, moving left to right, and then down to the next shelf. Once a bay is full, move to the bay on the right.

Books are shelved with spines facing outward and aligned with the edge of the shelf for neatness and easy reading of spine labels.

4. Arranging by Call Number

Use the library’s classification system (e.g., Dewey Decimal or Library of Congress) to determine the correct order.

For non-fiction, shelve books in numerical order by call number, reading digits after the decimal point one by one (not as a whole number).

For fiction, shelve alphabetically by the author’s last name or by collection code as indicated on the spine label.

5. Reshelving In-Use Books

Collect books left on tables, carrels, or return carts.

Add these to your cart and reshelve them in their correct locations during your shelving rounds.

6. Special Collections and Sizes

Reference, rare, oversized, and special collection books are shelved separately according to library policy.

Ensure these materials are placed in their designated areas.

7. Shelf Maintenance (Rectification)

Regularly check shelves for misplaced or misaligned books.

Straighten books, ensure correct order, and use bookends to keep them upright.

Tools Needed

Book carts, bookends, step stools, and sometimes ladders for upper shelves
AI Envision:
AI is transforming shelving and reshelving in libraries by automating repetitive, labor-intensive tasks and increasing efficiency. Robotic systems like Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (ASRS) can store and retrieve books without human intervention, using computer-controlled robots to locate and move items based on their database locations. Mobile robots equipped with RFID scanners, such as Singapore’s AuRoSS, autonomously scan shelves, detect misplaced or missing books, and even suggest the most efficient shelving routes, saving thousands of staff hours and reducing errors.

AI-driven systems also automate cataloging and classification, ensuring books are accurately organized and easy to find. These technologies free up library staff to focus on more complex, user-centered services, while improving the speed, accuracy, and accessibility of library collections

Understand the Requirements – I start by reviewing what the platform or client needs: what kind of media is being streamed (live or on-demand), target devices, quality levels, etc.

Set Up the Environment – I set up the dev environment using Visual Studio Code, and pull the latest code from GitHub.

Implement the Code – I write or modify the playback logic, ensuring the media player handles adaptive bitrate streaming, captions, and fallback options.

I review the client restrictions on each account, make sure there were no trades or securities placed into the accounts that violate the client's restrictions. I calculate share positions and reporting thresholds. There are short reporting thresholds for certain ESMA member countries. We have to make sure that if we cross those thresholds, we are prepared to make the filing. Calculate long positions for US filing thresholds and other international filing thresholds. I review personal trading for the employees, making sure all the trading is in accordance with our trading policies. I provide general advice to the employees and other staff. I review regulations, proposed regulations, current regulations to improve our current policies and procedures. I review marketing materials. I review investor subscription agreements, restriction letters, side letters, mark-up documents, make edits, and review and approve contracts.
Work Tasks: So I review trading reports from the previous day. These will indicate what our positions were at the end of the day coming into the new day. How close are we to filing thresholds? Have we gone over filing thresholds? Things like that. Just review general position size in the accounts. Are we getting close to our limits or not? Then there's also reviewing documents. There would be preparing for proxy votes, calculating the shares we've owned, getting approvals on how to vote, generally with management, but confirming that's the case because there are certain issues we might not vote with management. What do I do? Review and approve new accounts, investment accounts from clients, meaning subscription documents, side letters, review trading feeds for our employees, review and approve their personal trading requests, making sure they're in line with our policies, checking for blackout dates, meaning they're not trading in security within the time, within seven days of it being traded within one of our funds, checking allocations of the portfolios, just general things like that.
Work Tools: I use our OMS, order management system, to review current trades and historical trade activity. I can export the data using a SQL query. I can export the data into Excel and manipulate it there if I need to. We have EZE Compliance, which is a tool that allows us to enter different trading restrictions to monitor. It can monitor for allocations. It can restrict trading in certain securities. It can give alerts to the trading desk to seek further approval on certain trades. I use Excel to track shares or calculate shares, location of shares, which custodian shares are being held at for the proxy votes. I use Excel to keep track of violations, violation log, violation list, or other changes. Excel, the OMS, the compliance system, a calculator to manually calculate shares or positions at times. The Internet looking at current shares outstanding to confirm what the shares are. Sometimes when companies increase or decrease the shares, it's not quickly reflected in Bloomberg. Bloomberg tends to round to millions or 10 million. I don't remember exactly what it was. But sometimes that makes a difference if we're really close to a filing threshold we don't want to go over.
Work Processes: Okay, for portfolio monitoring and position reporting, it involves looking at the daily reports in the morning. These reports show what our positions were coming into the day at the end of yesterday, coming in today, how close we were to filing thresholds, the thresholds we were over and need to be filed in the morning. Once I have that information, I monitor for alerts throughout the day that we've crossed the threshold either above or under. Once we have that information, I pull the current shares outstanding or share capital, it's called in the UK, that would mean I'm going to the London Stock Exchange, search for the company's recent filings that show the share capital, and use that to figure out if we have a reportable position in that security. Similar process for ESMA securities, I'd have to go to the country's securities division and get the current share capital to confirm our filing position. Then there's reviewing personal, reviewing and approving personal trades. I review the trade request the employee has submitted, compare that to the securities that are in our holdings and if we trade within the past seven days to see if there's a conflict. For private offerings and other types of investments. I just review the details of the company they're looking to invest in, see if there's a conflict with our business and grant approval as needed. And I think a lot of the rest of the day is document review, rule review, things like that.

For personal trading, having a tool that compares the securities that the employees want to trade in to, you know, our recent trading activity for the past seven days to see if there are any conflicts there, that's great, I just have to speak to the portfolio managers and determine whether the funds will be trading in those securities in the next few days and then I can approve or deny as I need to. Work Description:
In my role as a robotics engineer, I’m involved in designing, building, and testing robotic systems. Most days, I work on both the mechanical and software sides of things. That includes using CAD software to design parts, writing code for the robot’s movement or behavior, and running tests to see how everything performs.

I also spend time troubleshooting any issues that come up, whether it’s a hardware malfunction or a bug in the control system. Teamwork is a big part of what I do, so I regularly collaborate with others to make sure all parts of the project are aligned. Some days are more hands-on with physical prototypes, while others are focused on planning, design, or documentation.

It’s a job that requires constant learning and creative thinking, but that’s part of what makes it exciting
Work Tasks:
Most of my time is spent on designing and programming. I’d say around 40–50% of my time goes into designing robotic parts using CAD tools and working on the control systems. About 30% is spent writing and testing code to control the robot’s movement or behavior.

The rest of the time is split between testing prototypes, troubleshooting hardware or software issues, and team meetings to discuss progress or solve problems. Sometimes I also help document the project or prepare updates for others on the team. It’s a good mix of hands-on work and computer-based tasks.
Work Tools:
The tools and software I use depend on the project, but some I use almost every day. The most frequent one is probably SolidWorks, which I use for designing mechanical parts and assemblies. I also use Python regularly for writing control algorithms and doing simulations.

Next would be MATLAB, which I use for analyzing data from tests and tuning control systems. For programming microcontrollers or robotics platforms, I often work with Arduino IDE or ROS (Robot Operating System), depending on the complexity of the system.

For collaboration and project management, I use tools like Slack, Trello, and Google Workspace. I also occasionally use Fusion 360, especially when working on quick prototyping or CAM-related tasks.
Work Processes:
One of the tasks I spend the most time on is designing robotic components and systems. Here’s how I usually go about it:

First, I start by understanding the requirements—what the robot needs to do, the environment it will operate in, and any constraints like size, weight, or budget. Then I sketch out some rough ideas, either on paper or using quick CAD drafts, to explore different concepts.

Once I have a solid direction, I move into SolidWorks to create detailed 3D models. I focus on making sure everything fits together properly and that the design is practical to manufacture. I often run simulations at this stage to check for stress, movement, or possible collisions.

After that, I review the design with teammates to get feedback. If everything looks good, I prepare the files for prototyping, which might involve 3D printing or CNC machining. Once the parts are made, I test the fit and function, then go back and tweak the design if needed based on what I learn from the prototype.

This process can take a few days or a few weeks depending on the complexity, but it’s a critical part of building functional and reliable robotic systems.
AI Envision:
I see AI becoming a really useful tool in my daily workflow, especially for speeding up repetitive or time-consuming tasks. For example, I could use AI to help with design optimization—generating multiple versions of a part based on performance requirements, which could save hours of manual modeling and testing.

AI could also support code debugging or auto-generating scripts for common control tasks, which would make the programming side faster and more efficient. I also think it could be helpful in data analysis—like interpreting test results from sensors or logs and giving quick insights or suggestions for adjustments.

Eventually, I’d like to integrate AI into the robotic systems themselves, like using computer vision models or reinforcement learning for smarter behavior. Overall, I see AI as a tool that complements my work, helping me move faster and make better decisions.

To do this, I design admission control strategies that incorporate real-time predictive information, such as anticipated future demand and processing rates, using techniques like Pontryagin's minimum principle. I then test these strategies using simulation experiments based on real hospital data to evaluate their effectiveness under various demand search scenarios. This includes formulating queuing models that represent patient flows in emergency departments, incorporating time-varying arrival patterns, service rates, and constraints like inpatient delays. I use techniques from fluid approximation and control theory to derive admission policies that are both interpretable and effective. About 30% of my time goes into data analysis and simulation, using real hospital data to calibrate arrival rates, estimate service times, and validate the assumptions in the models. I also design and run extensive numerical experiments to test how different policies perform under various demand surge scenarios. Roughly 20% of my time is spent on coding and implementation, especially building simulation environments, implementing policies, and integrating prediction models, like arrival forecasts, into the admission control framework. Work Processes: One of the tasks I spend the most time on is developing and analyzing mathematical models to improve emergency department operations. The process usually begins with understanding the operational context, such as how patients arrive, whether by ambulance, walk-in, or transfer, and how inpatient capacity limitations create bottlenecks. I'll start by reviewing hospital data and literature to define the key components of the system. Next, I move on to formalizing the problem using queuing theory. I write down the dynamics, such as arrival and service processes, server capacities, and admission delays using stochastic models. Since these models are often intractable, I then derive a fluid approximation, which allows me to work with continuous variables and differential equations. Once I have the fluid model, I apply optimal control techniques, like Pontryagin's minimum principle, to derive an admission policy that balances the trade-off between patient waiting times and the cost of rejecting transfer requests. I spend time interpreting the conditions under which we should accept or reject incoming patients based on real-time system congestion and predicted future demand. I prepare reports with similar properties explaining the rationale behind your valuation and I apply taxation codes ensuring property assessments are in line with local tax codes and regulation. And I handle disputes resolving disputes over property values providing evidence based on explanations and adjustments.
Work Tasks: I, as a real estate assessor, I do data collection, I gather information on properties including construction materials, age, improvements and the surrounding infrastructure. I do tax assessment, I calculate the property tax value based on the assessed value, ensuring it aligns with the local tax codes and regulation. I do market research by analysing local market trends, recent sales and other real estate data to compare and determine the property values. And I do customer interaction, this is by communicating with the property owners, real estate agents and sometimes legal professionals regarding property value and assessments. I do dispute resolution by addressing disputes related to property valuations, explaining the reason behind my assessment and making adjustments if necessary.
Work Tools: As a Real Estate Assessor, I use various tools, but the ones that I use mostly are QGIS. It's an open source GIS tool used for mapping and analysing spatial data related to properties. For real estate data platforms, I use CoreLogic, a tool for assessing property data, market trends and comparable sales information. And I use Spreadsheet Software, Microsoft Excel to be precise, used to organise, calculate property values and create reports. This is an essential tool in handling the large sums of data. And for property valuation tools, I use Landfolio. This is a software solution that supports land property valuation, assisting with GIS data assessments, calculations and report preparation.
Work Processes: For property inspections, I first prepare. Before heading out for a property inspection, I review the property's file and gather relevant information, such as the previous assessments and the property details. On site inspection, when I arrive at the property, I start by taking general photos of the exterior and the interior, focusing on the key areas, like the condition of the roof and foundations. I measure the property and notice any significant issues on the property. I gather details about surrounding neighbourhood. Then post inspection, this is the time when I go back to my office. I upload photos and notes into my assessment software or database. The second thing I do is market research. I collect sales data. This is, I start by reviewing sales from local MLS databases or other property platforms like Zillow or Realta.com. I analyse the comparable properties, I identify properties that have recently been sold or are currently on the market and look at factors that influence the pricing. Then I consider the market trends, then documentation, I record all the relevant data. Then preparing the report, I review the property details, I generate a report using Microsoft Word or Excel. Then I review and finalise, I proofread my report, then I submit the completed report to the relevant parties such as the local tax authority, property owners and clients for future reference.

Work Description:
I’m an indie video game designer and developer, mostly working solo or with my twin brother. I design and build games using Unreal Engine 5, with a heavy focus on Blueprints. Most of my games lean toward survival, RPG, or exploration themes. A lot of what I do daily depends on the current stage of development, but here’s how my typical workflow goes:

In the early stages, I focus on prototyping mechanics—things like movement systems, inventory, resource gathering, or AI behavior. I use Blueprints to build out logic for how characters interact with the world, including things like health, hunger systems, or NPC schedules.

Once core systems are stable, I move into level design. I create the environments in Unreal Engine using both marketplace assets and my own. That part includes terrain sculpting, lighting, placing structures, and making sure the player’s path feels interesting and purposeful. I also use Blender to model or tweak assets when needed.

When I’m not building or designing, I’m playtesting and optimizing. That could mean improving frame rates, fixing bugs, or rebalancing mechanics to feel right. I also do some sound design and UI work, like creating menus or tutorials, so players know how to play.
Work Tasks:
Gameplay Programming (40%)
Most of my time goes into using Unreal Engine Blueprints to build systems like movement, AI behavior, combat, UI interactions, or survival mechanics. I spend a lot of time tweaking logic, setting up events, and making sure everything works smoothly.

Level Design & World Building (25%)
I design the environments where the gameplay happens. That means placing structures, sculpting terrain, painting foliage, setting up lighting, and guiding the player’s path. This part takes a while because I want the world to feel alive and purposeful.

Playtesting & Debugging (15%)
After I add new systems or design updates, I test everything—playing through scenarios to catch bugs or weird behavior. I also try to optimize where I can by adjusting lighting settings, LODs, or performance-heavy assets.

Asset Management & Art Integration (10%)
I import and organize 3D models, animations, and textures—whether I’m using Marketplace content or something I’ve made in Blender. This also includes setting up materials and making sure things look good in-game.

Game Design & Planning (5%)
I brainstorm new mechanics, outline features, and plan progression systems or tutorials. I jot down ideas for future updates and features.

Sound & Music Integration (5%)
I place sound cues, ambient audio, and occasionally background music. I like experimenting with how sound can support player feedback and immersion, but it’s not a huge part of my workflow right now.
Work Tools:
Unreal Engine 5
This is my main workspace. I use it daily for pretty much everything—Blueprint scripting, level design, lighting, UI creation, animation setup, and testing. It's where I build and bring everything together.

Blender
I use Blender often, especially when I need custom models, animations, or to tweak assets. Sometimes I build full scenes here for stylized renders or game assets. It’s also my go-to for hair systems and environmental props.

hotoshop
I use Photoshop for editing textures, UI elements, and concept sketches. It's part of my pipeline for polishing visuals before importing them into Unreal.
Work Processes:
For Gameplay Programming :

I start by breaking down the feature I want to add—say, a hunger system or a villager’s daily routine. I think through what variables I need and how the logic should flow.

In Unreal Engine 5, I create Blueprint components or actors to keep things modular and clean. I’ll add variables, create custom events, and set up functions to handle different parts of the system.

I test as I go—usually by playing in-editor or simulating scenarios using debug print statements or breakpoints.

If something’s not working right, I troubleshoot by checking variable flow, cast failures, or event triggers. I also make sure to keep performance in mind—using event-driven logic instead of ticking whenever I can.

Once it works, I clean up the Blueprint, leave comments for future me (or my brother), and make sure it's easy to expand later.


For Level Design & World Building

First, I decide what part of the world I’m working on. I think about what the player should feel here—safe, tense, curious, etc.

I lay out key landmarks or structures using placeholder meshes, just to block things out.

Then I sculpt the terrain if needed, paint in layers like grass, dirt, or snow, and start adding foliage using the foliage tool.

I slowly replace placeholder meshes with detailed assets—either from the Marketplace, my own creations in Blender, or edited ones.

Once the environment feels right, I add lighting—directional, skylights, fog, maybe some post-processing.

I test traversal (especially if it’s a climbing or exploration section) to make sure everything feels smooth and intentional.

For Playtesting, Debugging

After I build a new feature or level, I jump into the game and play like a regular player. I try to "break" things—go places I shouldn’t, trigger stuff out of order, or leave villagers idle and see what happens.

I use print strings or "on-screen debug text" to see what’s going on under the hood in real time.

If something's off, I open the Blueprint or level and trace back where the issue starts. Sometimes I add temporary logs to find where a variable isn't updating or an event isn't firing.

Once fixed, I test again. I try edge cases—like what happens if the player runs out of food, or if a villager has no house.

When I’m confident it's stable, I remove debug prints and mark the task as complete in my notes.
AI Envision:
I don’t see AI as something that should fully automate my job—I love designing and building things myself—but I do see it as a powerful assistant that could make a lot of my daily work smoother and faster.

For example, I’d love an AI agent that could:

Catch logic issues in my Blueprints before I even press play—like warning me if a variable isn’t getting set or if an event chain might break.

Auto-generate filler dialogue for NPCs or tutorial messages based on context I give it, like “this is a villager explaining how food works.”

Help design more natural level flow by analyzing player movement and pointing out areas where people get stuck or confused.

Suggest optimizations for performance—like telling me if a light is casting shadows unnecessarily or if an actor’s tick could be replaced with a timer.

Organize my assets automatically, tagging models, textures, or Blueprints based on what they are and where they’re used.

Even small stuff, like generating placeholder quests, naming characters, or auto-filling a UI layout based on my inputs—that kind of support would save a ton of time and let me stay focused on creativity.

Overall, I’d want AI that understands my goals and works with me—almost like a smart teammate who can anticipate what I need next.

Business requirements which I learned in my step one, I use that to transform the data such that it is loaded into our target data warehouse.

Work Description: I conduct research on quantum information science and condensed matter physics. My research focuses on utilizing ultracold atoms and optical lattices to simulate complex quantum phenomena found in solid-state materials. Our experiments with ultracold atoms also offer valuable guidance in accelerating the development of neutral atom-based quantum computers.
Work Tasks: Our work mainly focus on creating optical lattices, reaching ultracool state of matter and finding new physics behind them. We will design experimental method such as the optical path for the ultracold atomic system.
Work Tools:
We use artiq to control the experiment. ARTIQ (Advanced Real-Time Infrastructure for Quantum physics) is a software framework designed for controlling quantum information experiments. 
ARTIQ is built by Python. The system includes hardware components such as FPGAs (Field-Programmable Gate Arrays) for managing precise timing and control, and it integrates with various devices commonly used in our labs, such as lasers, microwave sources, and photon detectors.
Work Processes:
1. Hardware Setup
Configure our experimental hardware: This includes all the physical devices like lasers, atom traps, detectors, and any other equipment used in my experiments.
Connect ARTIQ-compatible hardware: ARTIQ requires specific hardware that can interface with its control system, such as FPGAs and DACs (Digital-to-Analog Converters) for precise control and timing of the experimental procedures.

2. Experiment Design
Write the experiment code: Using Python extended with ARTIQ's libraries, write the code that defines the sequence of actions and timing for our experiment. This code specifies how and when to trigger lasers, collect data, and adjust parameters.
Compile and simulate: Before running on actual hardware, the experiment code can be compiled and simulated to check for logical or timing errors.

3. Deployment
Upload to hardware: Deploy the compiled experiment to the ARTIQ hardware. This involves loading the code onto the FPGAs or other control devices.
Run the experiment: Execute the experiment using the ARTIQ dashboard or command-line tools, which allow me to monitor the experiment's progress and make real-time adjustments if necessary.
AI Envision: It can help me write some basic codes to control the hardware. I suppose that we can also use the genAI to help us conduct experiment. We can give some instructions on how to change the parameters in the experiment. * ILS (Integrated Library System): Core software managing cataloging (record creation), circulation (borrowing/lending), acquisitions (ordering), serials (journals), and the OPAC (user search interface). Examples: Koha, FOLIO, Alma.
 * Discovery Layers: Enhanced search interfaces overlaying the OPAC, providing broader resource discovery and improved user experience. Examples: EBSCO Discovery Service, Summon.
 * Digital Library Software/IRs: Platforms for managing and providing access to digital collections (documents, media). Examples: DSpace, Fedora.
 * ERM (Electronic Resource Management): Systems tracking e-resource licenses, access, and usage statistics.
 * Reference Management Software: Tools for organizing research, creating citations and bibliographies (used by librarians and patrons). Examples: Zotero, Mendeley.
 * ILL Management Systems: Facilitate interlibrary loan requests between libraries. Example: OCLC WorldShare ILL.
Work Processes:
Assisting Patrons (Reference):
 * Greet & understand the need.
 * Clarify the research question.
 * Identify relevant resources.
 * Guide to resources & search strategies.
 * Help evaluate results & offer further aid.
Checking Out Materials (Circulation):
 * Identify patron (library card).
 * Scan items.
 * Review items & due dates.
 * Process checkout in system.
Cataloging a New Book:
 * Identify the book.
 * Search for existing records.
 * Create/adapt bibliographic record (title, author, etc.).
 * Assign subject headings & classification number.
 * Create item record (barcode, location).
 * Review and add to shelves.

-Nanofabrication Equipment: (e.g., lithography systems, thin film deposition tools, etching systems) for building nanoscale devices.
-Simulation and Modeling Software: (e.g., COMSOL, ANSYS, MATLAB) for simulating nanoscale behavior and designing systems.

Work Processes:
-Define the Problem/Need: Identify the specific issue or application the nanosystem aims to address.
-Conceptualize a Solution: Brainstorm and develop ideas for a nanosystem that can solve the problem.
-Design at the Nanoscale: Specify the materials, structures, and functionalities required at the atomic and molecular level.
-Model and Simulate: Use software to predict the behavior and performance of the designed nanosystem.
-Fabricate the Nanocomponents: Utilize nanofabrication techniques to build the individual elements of the system.
-Characterize the Nanomaterials/Devices: Employ microscopy and spectroscopy to analyze the properties of the fabricated components.
-Integrate Components: Assemble the individual nanoscale parts into a functional larger system (often microscale or macroscale).
-Test and Evaluate: Assess the performance of the integrated nanosystem against the initial requirements.
-Optimize and Iterate: Refine the design and fabrication process based on testing results to improve performance.
-Implement and Apply: Deploy the developed nanosystem for its intended application.

Work Description: As a Customs Broker, my day-to-day work involves ensuring the smooth import and export of goods across international borders. I handle customs documentation, classify goods according to tariff codes, and make sure all shipments comply with local and international regulations. I work closely with clients, government agencies, freight forwarders, and carriers to resolve any customs issues and ensure timely clearance of goods. A big part of my job also includes staying updated on trade laws, tariffs, and regulatory changes to keep everything compliant and efficient.
Work Tasks:
Document preparation and review (30%): Preparing and verifying import/export documentation, including commercial invoices, packing lists, and customs declarations.
Customs clearance and compliance (25%): Classifying goods, ensuring compliance with relevant regulations, and submitting entries to customs systems.
Client communication and coordination (20%): Communicating with clients to gather shipment details, provide updates, and address any issues or delays.
Liaising with government agencies and carriers (15%): Working with customs officials, freight forwarders, and transportation providers to ensure smooth processing of shipments.
Research and regulatory updates (10%): Staying informed about changing trade laws, tariffs, and customs requirements to maintain compliance and advise clients.
Work Tools: I use a mix of systems and tools throughout the day, depending on the task. Most frequently, I work with customs declaration platforms like ACE or CADEX to submit entries and track shipments. Our team also uses internal brokerage software (like Descartes or Cargowise) to manage shipments, client details, and classification codes. Email is a big part of my day—I’m constantly in touch with clients, customs officials, and freight forwarders. I also refer to tariff classification tools and government websites to make sure everything’s compliant. For documents, I usually use Excel and Adobe to review and organize invoices, packing lists, and supporting paperwork.
Work Processes:
One of the tasks I spend the most time on is preparing and filing customs entries for imported goods. The process usually starts with reviewing the shipment documents—things like the commercial invoice, packing list, and bill of lading. I check for accuracy and make sure everything matches up.

Next, I determine the correct HS codes and check for any special requirements, like permits, duties, or restrictions. Once I’ve classified the goods, I enter all the necessary information into our brokerage system or directly into a customs platform like ACE or CADEX.

After submission, I monitor the status of the entry to make sure it clears without issues. If customs flags something or requests more info, I follow up quickly—either with the agency or the client—to resolve it. Once the goods are cleared, I finalize the documentation and confirm delivery or release with the freight forwarder or warehouse.

It’s a detailed process that requires a lot of accuracy and coordination, so I’ve developed a good rhythm over time.
AI Envision:
I can see AI being really helpful in streamlining some of the more repetitive parts of my workflow. For example, automatically extracting and checking data from shipping documents like invoices and packing lists would save a lot of time and reduce errors. AI could also assist with HS code suggestions based on product descriptions, which would speed up classification.

Another area where AI could help is by monitoring regulatory changes and flagging relevant updates, so I can stay compliant without spending hours researching. Even something as simple as summarizing long email threads or generating quick responses could make daily communication more efficient.

Overall, I think AI has the potential to reduce manual work and help me focus more on problem-solving and client support.

Work Tasks:
As a Precision Agriculture Technician, my work involves a mix of fieldwork, data analysis, and equipment maintenance. Here's how I usually divide my time:

Field Data Collection and Monitoring (30-35%)
I spend a large part of my time out in the field gathering data using GPS devices, soil sensors, drones, and other remote sensing tools to assess crop health, soil conditions, and moisture levels.

Data Analysis and Mapping (25-30%)
After collecting data, I use software like GIS, precision ag platforms, and mapping tools to analyze field conditions. This helps identify patterns and make recommendations on seeding rates, fertilizer application, and irrigation.

Equipment Setup and Maintenance (20-25%)
I install, calibrate, and maintain precision ag systems on farm equipment like tractors, planters, and sprayers to ensure accurate operations. I also troubleshoot any technical issues that come up.

Reporting and Decision Support (10-15%)
I generate reports for farmers and agronomists to help them make informed decisions. This includes yield predictions, input suggestions, and technology recommendations based on data analysis.

Training and Support (5-10%)
I sometimes assist farmers or coworkers in learning how to use new technologies, software, or equipment properly.

Every season brings different priorities, but these core tasks make up the majority of my role in supporting modern, data-driven farming.

GPS and RTK Systems (Used Daily)

Examples: Trimble, John Deere GPS, Ag Leader
These are essential for collecting accurate location data and guiding machinery for precise planting, spraying, and harvesting.

GIS and Mapping Software (Used Frequently)

Examples: ArcGIS, Ag Leader SMS, Climate FieldView
I use these platforms to create field maps, analyze spatial data, track input applications, and generate variable rate prescriptions.

Sensor and Monitoring Equipment (Used Frequently)

Examples: Soil moisture sensors, NDVI sensors, weather stations
These tools help collect real-time field data related to soil health, crop stress, and environmental conditions.

Drone and Aerial Imaging Tools (Used Regularly)

Examples: DJI drones, DroneDeploy, Pix4D
I use drones to capture high-resolution images and monitor large field areas for signs of pests, disease, or nutrient deficiencies.

Machinery Control Systems (Used Regularly During Field Operations)

Examples: John Deere Operations Center, Raven Viper, Case IH AFS
These systems are used to control variable rate technology (VRT), auto-steering, and application monitors on farming equipment.

Data Management and Cloud Platforms (Used Weekly or As Needed)

Examples: Granular, AgFiniti, MyJohnDeere
These help manage and store collected field data, sync devices, and share insights with farmers and agronomists.

These tools work together to improve efficiency, accuracy, and decision-making in the field.
Work Processes:
One of the tasks I spend the most time on is collecting and analyzing field data to help optimize crop performance. Here’s how I typically approach it:

Planning and Setup
I start by identifying the fields or zones that need to be monitored based on crop stage, client needs, or prior data. I prepare equipment such as GPS units, soil probes, sensors, or drones, and ensure everything is calibrated and synced.

Field Data Collection
I go out to the fields and collect various types of data—this could include GPS-tagged soil samples, NDVI images from drones, moisture levels from sensors, or crop stand counts. I make sure to follow consistent sampling methods for accurate comparisons.

Data Transfer and Organization
After collecting the data, I upload it to a computer or cloud platform like Climate FieldView, Ag Leader SMS, or ArcGIS. I organize it by field, crop type, and date, ensuring everything is labeled correctly for analysis.

Data Cleaning and Analysis
I review the data to check for inconsistencies or errors. Then I analyze it to identify trends—such as low-yielding zones, signs of nutrient deficiency, or areas with poor drainage. I use mapping software to visualize this information.

Create Recommendations and Reports
Based on the insights, I generate maps and reports to support decisions on variable rate seeding, fertilization, or irrigation. I often collaborate with farmers or agronomists to explain the findings and help them adjust their strategies.

Follow-Up and Monitoring
I sometimes revisit fields after recommendations are implemented to gather follow-up data and see how well the changes worked. This helps refine future decisions and improves long-term results.

This task is central to what I do because it directly connects the technology to better farming outcomes.

Predictive Insights
With historical and real-time data, AI can generate predictive models for yield forecasting, pest outbreaks, or irrigation needs. This allows us to move from being reactive to proactive in managing field conditions.

Automated Recommendations
AI could assist in generating variable rate prescriptions for seeding, fertilizing, and spraying based on multi-layered field data. This would reduce the time I spend manually creating maps and improve precision.

Machine Learning for Equipment Tuning
AI systems in tractors and sprayers could self-optimize based on terrain, crop type, and weather conditions—improving input accuracy and reducing waste.

Faster Reporting and Decision Support
AI tools could automatically compile field reports, highlight key metrics, and even suggest next steps, making it easier to provide clear, actionable insights to farmers and agronomists.

Work Processes:
As a Network Engineer, I spend most of my time on troubleshooting connectivity issues. Here’s how I typically handle it:

Identify the Problem – I start by gathering details from users or monitoring tools to understand the symptoms (e.g., no internet, slow speeds, or service downtime).

Initial Checks – I verify physical connections, device status (ping test), and confirm if it's a localized or widespread issue.

Use Diagnostic Tools – I run commands like ping, traceroute, nslookup, or use Wireshark to analyze traffic flow and detect anomalies.

Isolate the Issue – I check switch/router configs, firewall rules, or VLAN setups to pinpoint where the problem lies.

Apply a Fix – Once identified, I make necessary changes—like adjusting routes, replacing faulty hardware, or rebooting services.

Test & Confirm – I test the fix, monitor the system for stability, and confirm with the user that everything is resolved.

Document – Finally, I log the issue, resolution steps, and update documentation to help with future troubleshooting.

Analysis & Interpretation: I use tools like Excel, Prosper, and simulation software (e.g., CMG or Eclipse) to perform decline curve analysis, nodal analysis, or reservoir simulation. The goal here is to understand current well performance and predict future output.

Scenario Modeling: Based on the analysis, I may run different production or development scenarios—for example, evaluating the impact of artificial lift, infill drilling, or workovers.

Recommendation Development: I then summarize my findings and develop recommendations—whether that’s optimizing choke settings, proposing stimulation, or flagging underperforming wells.
